point_001: The paper lacks clarity on how inputs and outputs are reformulated for each of the three tasks (entity disambiguation, entity linking, document retrieval), and baseline systems are insufficiently described.
point_002: The decoding procedure for end-to-end entity linking is not clearly explained; a formal description (e.g., formula) of how log-probabilities are computed would improve understanding.
point_003: There is no analysis of performance on tail/rare entities, which is important because (a) larger entity vocabularies will contain more low-frequency entities, and (b) autoregressive models lack the implicit training signal that contrastive/negative-sampling methods provide for all entities.
point_004: The contribution of constrained trie-based beam search versus unconstrained beam decoding is not ablated; even one or two data points showing the performance impact would clarify how critical this component is.
point_005: It is unclear how much of the performance gain stems from large-scale pretraining versus the proposed autoregressive method itself; ablation results (Tables 7 and 8) should be moved to the main paper with clearer explanations.
point_006: The model is limited to entities that have Wikipedia pages and relies heavily on copying surface forms, raising questions about how it would handle rare or out-of-Wikipedia entities.
point_007: Inference-time efficiency is not compared against retrieval methods that use Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS) over dense entity representations, making it hard to assess practical computational trade-offs.
point_008: The appendix contains result tables without inline explanations, reducing the paper's overall clarity and accessibility.
point_009: Additional analysis on what entity characteristics (name length, popularity, ambiguity) correlate with model success or failure—and whether GENRE is complementary to existing methods—would strengthen the empirical contribution.
